name: Arroz CON Gandules Wepa!
intro: Most cultures have traditional rice. For me, it is arroz con gandules, which translates to “rice with pigeon peas.” It is the rice of Puerto Rico, usually made with ham meat and chicken bouillon. I grew up eating this rice with everything. Arroz con gandules can be a little intimidating to make, but with some practice, patience and attention, it can be made perfectly every time. I remember crying on my 24th birthday because I needed to bring arroz con gandules to a barbecue and I didn’t know how to make it. I called my mom to do it for me, but she couldn’t because she was at the hospital with my uncle, which frustrated me. I broke down, and after the waterworks, I went into the kitchen and started to make it how my family did. It came out pretty good, and ever since that day, I made sure to practice. I will never forget the day my Titi Baby Evelyn tried my rice, and she came up to me and told me it tasted better than my mom’s. It made me feel so proud to finally master the rice of my island, and I take pride in making it all the time now, but vegan. The small substitutes still pack so much flavor that you’re not missing anything. I hope this recipe makes you feel proud and makes your soul happy, just as it did for me.

ingredients.item: Better Than Bouillon® organic vegetable bouillon

method: In your caldero (lidded rice pot) over medium heat, combine the olive oil, sofrito, garlic, vegetable bouillon, olives, culantro, salt, adobo, Sazón and ½ cup (120 ml) of the water. Mix everything with a spoon and simmer for about 5 minutes.
Add the gandules and bay leaves and simmer for an additional 3 minutes while mixing occasionally. Add your rice to the pot, and stir well. Taste to ensure the seasoning is to your liking.

Once mixed, add the remaining 4 cups (980 ml) of water, mix everything together thoroughly and cover the rice with the banana leaves (if using). The banana leaves add moisture and an herblike aroma to the rice. Allow the rice to cook over medium heat, covered with a lid, for about 15 minutes.

Check the rice and make sure it is being cooked evenly. Once you see the water is absorbed, lower the heat to low and cover. Cook for an additional 7 to 10 minutes, or until the rice is soft and fluffy. Fluff the rice with a fork. At this point, it should be nice and soft, and everything evenly cooked. If not, leave the pot covered until the rice is tender. If you find the bay leaves, you can remove them. Otherwise, I just eat around them. Once the rice is done, plate it! ¡Buen provecho!
